key_points: Climate refers to the average weather conditions of a place over a long period of time
Weather refers to the condition of the atmosphere at a particular place and time
The main elements of weather and climate include temperature, atmospheric pressure, wind, humidity and precipitation
India has a monsoon type of climate due to seasonal reversal of winds
Climatic controls include latitude, altitude, pressure and wind systems, distance from the sea, ocean currents and relief features
The Himalayas play an important role by blocking cold winds from Central Asia
Temperature decreases with increase in altitude which is why hill stations are cooler than plains
Places near the sea experience moderate climate due to the influence of the ocean
Monsoon winds bring most of the rainfall to India
Rainfall distribution in India varies greatly from region to region
examples: Example of heavy rainfall region: Mawsynram in Meghalaya
Example of low rainfall region: Western Rajasthan
Example of orographic rainfall: Khasi Hills in Meghalaya
Example of hot winds: Loo winds in northern India
Example of winter rainfall: Western disturbances in northern India
formulas: Climate = Average weather conditions over a long period
Orographic rainfall = Rainfall caused by mountains forcing air to rise
Continentality = Extreme climate due to distance from the sea
exam_tips: Remember the difference between weather and climate
Learn the six climatic controls affecting India's climate
Understand the four seasons of India: cold weather, hot weather, advancing monsoon and retreating monsoon
Remember important examples such as Mawsynram for highest rainfall
Focus on the importance of monsoon for agriculture and economy
